agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding a driver-facing earnings breakdown feature across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal frameworks governing driver classification, compensation transparency obligations, and approval processes. This is not a single feature — it is three jurisdiction-specific implementations sharing a common UI concept.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Earnings / Compensation Transparency",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The fixed compensation component per delivery or per hour, before bonuses, tips, or deductions. For employees (CA), this is the hourly wage. For contractors (ID), this is the per-task base rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to completing a specific delivery, often influenced by distance, demand surge, or time-of-day factors." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "Voluntary gratuity from the customer to the driver. Must never be commingled with base pay or bonuses in any jurisdiction." },
      { "term": "Deduction (Employee)", "definition": "In employee jurisdictions (CA): legally mandated withholdings (tax, social security) and authorized voluntary deductions (benefits). Each must be individually itemized with legal basis." },
      { "term": "Deduction (Contractor)", "definition": "In contractor jurisdictions (ID): platform service fees, commissions, or equipment charges. These are not wage deductions — they are contractual cost-sharing. Must be labeled differently to avoid misclassification risk." },
      { "term": "Itemized Wage Statement", "definition": "California Labor Code §226 mandates an accurate itemized statement each pay period showing gross wages, total hours, deductions, net wages, pay period dates, and employer identity." },
      { "term": "Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "German employee representative body with co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1)(6) over introduction of technical systems that monitor employee performance or behavior, including compensation display systems." },
      { "term": "Co-determination Gate", "definition": "A blocking approval step requiring formal Betriebsrat consent before a feature can be deployed in Germany. Deployment without this consent is legally void and exposes the platform to injunctions." },
      { "term": "Net Earnings", "definition": "The final amount payable to the driver after all additions and deductions. Must always equal the sum: base_pay + bonuses + tips - deductions." },
      { "term": "Real-Time Earnings", "definition": "Earnings data updated and visible to the driver within the current work session, reflecting completed deliveries. Does NOT mean sub-second streaming — means 'current and not delayed until end of pay period'." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "The displayed net earnings MUST always equal base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - deductions. No rounding or display error may cause a discrepancy.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Arithmetic integrity of financial data. If the breakdown does not sum to the total, the system is displaying corrupt financial information. This is a data consistency constraint, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every earnings record displayed, assert that SUM(base_pay, bonuses, tips) - SUM(deductions) == displayed_net_earnings, with zero tolerance."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Tips must never be included in or offset against base pay or bonus calculations in any jurisdiction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tip misappropriation violates labor law in California (Labor Code §351) and would constitute fraud against contractors in Indonesia. This is a universal financial integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that removing all tip records from a pay period does not change the base_pay or per_delivery_bonus values."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, every earnings display MUST include: gross wages earned, total hours worked, each deduction individually itemized with description, net wages, and inclusive dates of the pay period — per Labor Code §226.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal mandate. Failure to comply exposes the platform to statutory penalties of $250 per violation per employee per pay period, uncapped in aggregate. The system would be operating unlawfully.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any CA driver, assert the earnings view contains all six §226-mandated fields and that no deduction appears without an accompanying description string."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The Germany deployment MUST NOT go live until a formal Betriebsrat approval artifact is recorded in the system. This is a hard deployment gate, not a soft notification.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under BetrVG §87(1)(6), deploying a technical monitoring/display system without co-determination is legally void. A court can issue an injunction to shut it down and the company faces liability. This is not a policy preference — it is a legal precondition to operation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the feature flag for Germany earnings transparency cannot be enabled unless a works_council_approval record exists with status=APPROVED and a date on or before the activation date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Deductions for employees (CA, DE) and platform fees for contractors (ID) must use distinct labeling and never share the same UI terminology or data model field name.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Using the term 'deduction' for contractor platform fees creates worker misclassification risk. If a contractor's earnings screen looks like an employee wage statement, it can be used as evidence of de facto employment in litigation. This is a legal/structural constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the earnings breakdown for ID contractors never contains the string 'deduction' and instead uses 'platform fee', 'service fee', or equivalent contractor-appropriate language."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "California drivers must see earnings data updated within the current work session (not delayed to end-of-pay-period). Each completed delivery should reflect in the breakdown before the next delivery is accepted.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The update frequency is a product/UX policy. California requires itemized statements per pay period, not per delivery. Real-time display is a product commitment, not a legal mandate. A VP could decide to batch-update hourly instead.",
      "testable_assertion": "After a delivery is marked complete in CA, assert the driver's earnings view reflects the new delivery within 60 seconds."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Each line item in the earnings breakdown must be attributable to a specific delivery or time period. No opaque lump sums.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a transparency policy decision, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide that daily summaries are sufficient rather than per-delivery attribution. However, in CA, §226 requires enough specificity to be 'accurate' and 'itemized'.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that every non-zero earnings line item references either a delivery_id or a date_range."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Germany earnings display must comply with Betriebsrat-approved format. If the works council mandates specific fields be hidden, added, or reordered, the Germany variant must respect those conditions.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Co-determination means the works council can impose binding conditions on the feature's form, not just approve/deny. Ignoring conditions violates the agreement and voids the approval.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the Germany earnings view configuration is loaded from a jurisdiction-specific template that can be independently modified without affecting CA or ID views."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A California driver has completed 3 deliveries in the current session",
      "when": "The driver opens the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "All 3 deliveries are listed with individual base pay, bonus, tip, and itemized deductions (with descriptions), total hours, gross wages, net wages, and pay period dates — all §226-compliant"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The Germany Betriebsrat has NOT yet approved the compensation display feature",
      "when": "A product manager attempts to enable the feature flag for Germany",
      "then": "The system rejects activation and surfaces an error: 'Works council approval required before deployment'"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The Germany Betriebsrat has approved the feature with the condition that individual delivery-level tip amounts are hidden (only showing a daily tip total)",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "Tips are displayed as a daily aggregate, not per-delivery, matching the works council's approved format"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "An Indonesian contractor driver views their earnings",
      "when": "The platform has charged a 20% service fee on a delivery",
      "then": "The fee is displayed as 'Platform Service Fee' (not 'Deduction'), and the breakdown shows: base rate, bonus, tip, platform service fee, and net payout"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver in any jurisdiction has an earnings record displayed",
      "when": "The individual line items are summed",
      "then": "The computed total exactly matches the displayed net earnings with zero discrepancy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A California driver completes a delivery and receives a $5 tip",
      "when": "The next earnings breakdown is generated",
      "then": "The $5 tip appears as a separate line item and the base pay and bonus amounts are identical to what they would have been with no tip"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A California driver is in an active session",
      "when": "A delivery is marked complete by the system",
      "then": "The earnings breakdown reflects the new delivery within 60 seconds without requiring a manual refresh"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "The platform operates in all three jurisdictions",
      "when": "The earnings feature is deployed",
      "then": "Each jurisdiction renders from an independent, jurisdiction-specific template — a change to the CA template does not alter the DE or ID view"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A delivery is completed but the tip is added by the customer 30 minutes later", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown initially shows $0 tip for that delivery. When the tip arrives, the line item updates in-place and the net total recalculates. The historical record must show the tip was added post-delivery, not backdated." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver works a shift that crosses midnight (two pay periods)", "expected_behavior": "Hours and earnings are correctly split across the two pay periods. Each pay period's §226 statement is independently valid and complete." },
    { "scenario": "A deduction is reversed (e.g., erroneous equipment charge refunded)", "expected_behavior": "The reversal appears as a separate positive line item referencing the original deduction, not a silent deletion. Audit trail is preserved." },
    { "scenario": "Germany works council approval is revoked after the feature is live", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately deactivatable in Germany without affecting CA or ID. A kill-switch per jurisdiction is required." },
    { "scenario": "An Indonesian driver disputes a platform service fee", "expected_behavior": "The earnings screen provides enough detail (delivery ID, fee percentage, fee basis) for the driver to identify and contest specific charges. A dispute flow must be reachable from the fee line item." },
    { "scenario": "Currency and locale differences across jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "CA displays in USD, DE in EUR, ID in IDR. All amounts formatted per locale conventions (decimal separators, currency symbols). No currency conversion — amounts are native to the jurisdiction." },
    { "scenario": "A driver relocates from Indonesia to California", "expected_behavior": "The driver's classification changes from contractor to employee. Historical Indonesian earnings remain labeled with contractor terminology. New CA earnings use employee terminology and §226 compliance from the reclassification date forward." },
    { "scenario": "Zero-earnings pay period (driver was active but all deliveries were cancelled)", "expected_behavior": "CA still requires a §226 statement showing $0 gross wages and 0 hours. The system must not skip statement generation for zero-earnings periods." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "California non-compliance with §226 carries penalties of $250/violation/employee/pay period (first offense), escalating to $1,000 for subsequent violations, uncapped in class actions. Germany deployment without Betriebsrat approval risks injunctions halting all operations, not just this feature. Correct implementation builds driver trust, reducing churn — driver acquisition costs in logistics typically run $300-$1,500 per driver.",
    "operational": "Three jurisdiction-specific templates increase maintenance surface area. The Germany co-determination gate introduces a non-engineering dependency on the deployment timeline. Real-time earnings calculation requires robust event-driven architecture — eventual consistency bugs will surface as driver complaints to support. Expect 15-25% increase in support tickets during rollout if deduction/fee labeling is confusing.",
    "user": "Drivers gain unprecedented visibility into their compensation, directly increasing trust and perceived fairness. In CA, this is table-stakes legal compliance. In ID, this is a competitive differentiator — most platforms show only net payout. Poorly labeled deductions or arithmetic discrepancies will destroy trust faster than no transparency at all.",
    "risk": "Highest risk is misclassification exposure in Indonesia: if the contractor earnings screen mimics an employee wage statement, it becomes plaintiff's exhibit A in a reclassification lawsuit. Second highest risk is Germany deployment without Betriebsrat approval — this is an injunction-level violation. Third is California §226 non-compliance at scale, which is a class-action magnet.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Gain legally mandated wage transparency. Empowered to verify correct pay. May surface existing payroll errors that were previously invisible." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Feature delayed pending works council approval. If approved with conditions, may see a more restricted view than other jurisdictions." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Gain visibility into platform fees for the first time. Likely to increase fee-related disputes initially, but builds long-term trust and retention." },
      { "group": "Works Council (Germany)", "impact": "Must be engaged as a co-decision-maker, not merely notified. Requires dedicated legal/HR coordination. Timeline is outside engineering control." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate §226 compliance for CA, review contractor language for ID misclassification risk, and manage Betriebsrat process for DE. This is a multi-month cross-functional effort." },
      { "group": "Finance/Payroll", "impact": "Real-time earnings display creates a de facto audit trail visible to drivers. Any discrepancies between displayed amounts and actual payouts will be immediately flagged. Payroll accuracy must be airtight." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive increased volume of earnings-related inquiries during rollout. Must be trained on jurisdiction-specific terminology and escalation paths." },
      { "group": "Product/Engineering", "impact": "Must build a jurisdiction-aware architecture from day one. Retrofitting jurisdiction logic into a monolithic earnings view will create compounding technical debt." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence requesting a uniform feature across three jurisdictions with fundamentally incompatible legal frameworks. It does not acknowledge that 'deductions' means different things for employees vs contractors, does not mention the Betriebsrat approval gate as a deployment blocker, and treats real-time display as a simple UI addition when it has significant legal and architectural implications. The ambiguity around the term 'deductions' and the missing legal/regulatory review for three distinct labor law regimes drive confidence below 55.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Arithmetic integrity of earnings breakdown (BR-1) is unambiguous",
      "Tip separation from base pay (BR-2) is universally applicable",
      "Germany Betriebsrat gate is a well-established legal requirement",
      "California §226 requirements are codified in statute"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact deduction categories required for §226 compliance in California — needs legal review",
      "Betriebsrat conditions — cannot predict what the works council will require, may fundamentally alter the DE feature shape",
      "Indonesia contractor fee disclosure obligations — local law analysis not provided",
      "Whether 'real time' means per-delivery, per-hour, or per-session — spec is silent",
      "Tax withholding display requirements per jurisdiction"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "California drivers are classified as W-2 employees under AB5, not exempted under Prop 22 (Prop 22 was ruled unconstitutional but legal status is contested — this MUST be confirmed with legal)",
      "Germany operations have an established Betriebsrat — if no works council exists at the German entity, co-determination rules differ",
      "Indonesian contractor agreements include a contractual basis for platform fees that can be displayed",
      "The platform already has the underlying payroll/payment data in sufficient granularity to power a per-delivery breakdown"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec uses the term 'deductions' uniformly across all three jurisdictions, but this term has critically different legal meanings: wage deductions for employees (CA, DE) vs. platform/service fees for contractors (ID). Using 'deductions' for Indonesian contractors creates misclassification risk. The spec must explicitly define what 'deductions' means per jurisdiction and per driver classification.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Drivers (Indonesia)", "Product"],
      "options": [
        "A: Use 'deductions' only for employee jurisdictions; use 'platform fees' or 'service charges' for contractor jurisdictions",
        "B: Use a neutral term like 'adjustments' everywhere and let jurisdiction-specific templates control labeling",
        "C: Define a domain glossary per jurisdiction and enforce it at the API and UI layer"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — explicit terminology split is the safest approach for misclassification defense. Option B creates ambiguity; Option C is correct but may be over-engineered for three jurisdictions."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'real time' but does not define the latency tolerance. For California §226 compliance, per-pay-period is the legal minimum. For driver trust, per-delivery is the expectation. These have vastly different architectural implications (batch vs event-driven). The product owner must specify the update frequency commitment per jurisdiction.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Engineering", "Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Per-delivery update within 60 seconds of delivery completion (event-driven)",
        "B: Per-session summary updated every 15 minutes (near-real-time batch)",
        "C: Per-pay-period statement only (legal minimum for CA, insufficient for 'real time' promise)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for the earnings view, with Option C as the compliance artifact. Drivers see per-delivery updates; the §226 statement is generated per pay period as a separate compliance document."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "California AB5 / Proposition 22 status for this platform's drivers is not specified. Prop 22 exempted app-based drivers from AB5 employee classification but was ruled unconstitutional (Castellanos v. State, 2021). The case is under appeal. Whether this platform's CA drivers are employees or contractors TODAY materially changes every business rule. Legal counsel must confirm current classification.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Drivers (California)", "Finance/Payroll"],
      "options": [
        "A: Assume employee classification (conservative — build for §226 compliance)",
        "B: Assume Prop 22 contractor classification (risky — may need to retrofit if appeal fails)",
        "C: Build both paths and feature-flag by classification status"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — assume employee classification. It is the more demanding compliance path, and if Prop 22 is ultimately upheld, the feature gracefully degrades (employees get more transparency than required for contractors, which is not a violation). The reverse is not true."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Germany Betriebsrat timeline and conditions are unknown and outside engineering control. The feature cannot ship in Germany without this approval, and the approval may impose conditions that fundamentally alter the feature's shape (e.g., hiding certain fields, requiring anonymization of comparative data). Product must engage HR/Legal to initiate the Betriebsrat process BEFORE engineering begins the DE variant.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Works Council (Germany)", "Legal/Compliance", "Product", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Build the DE variant speculatively and adjust after Betriebsrat feedback",
        "B: Build CA and ID first; begin DE only after Betriebsrat conditions are known",
        "C: Build a configurable template system that can accommodate unknown Betriebsrat conditions"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C with Option B's phasing — build a jurisdiction-configurable template system for CA and ID first, then apply it to DE once Betriebsrat conditions are known. This avoids throwaway work while maintaining architecture readiness."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
